realWorldScenario: Diego asks the AI for the population of his hometown in Argentina. The answer comes back with a precise number and zero hedging. Diego, who grew up there, knows the real number is roughly half what the AI said. The AI was confident. The AI was wrong.

questions.question: What's the BEST mental rule for reading any AI response?
options: If it sounds confident, it's true
Treat confidence as a style, not a measure of accuracy — verify what matters
Trust everything as long as it's well-formatted
Only trust short answers
questions.explanation: AI is trained to sound confident regardless of whether it's right. Confidence is a property of the writing style, not the truth. For anything that matters, verify.

airaFeedback.correct: That distinction — confidence is style, not truth — will protect you for years.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Confidence in AI is an interface choice, not a sign of accuracy. The AI sounds the same whether it's right or completely making things up.

questions.question: If the AI gives you specific numbers, dates, or quotes, you should always double-check them before relying on them.
questions.explanation: Specific facts — numbers, dates, names, quotes, citations — are the easiest places for AI to hallucinate confidently. They're also the highest-stakes things to get wrong. Always verify.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Specifics are where confident hallucinations live. Check the small things.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Numbers, dates, and quotes are exactly where AI quietly invents things while sounding 100% sure. Always verify them.

questions.question: Which kind of AI claim deserves the MOST skepticism?
options: A general definition of a common word
A specific statistic, date, or quote attributed to a real person
An obvious opinion about taste
A summary of a famous book
questions.explanation: The most dangerous claims are specific, attributable, and verifiable in principle but rarely verified in practice. That's exactly where confident invention happens — and the cost of being wrong is highest.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. The more verifiable a claim is, the more important it is to actually verify it.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Specific facts attributed to real sources — that's where AI invention is most dangerous, because it sounds the most authoritative.

questions.question: AI confidence is a property of the writing _____, not the truth.
questions.explanation: AI is trained to write smoothly and authoritatively. That style sounds the same whether the underlying claim is right or completely fabricated.

airaFeedback.correct: Style. Once you see it, you can't unsee it.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Think about what makes confident writing FEEL true even when it isn't — it's the way it's written, not what it says.

questions.question: Write a follow-up prompt you'd send to an AI to pressure-test a confident factual claim it just made.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'How sure are you about that specific claim? What's your source, and what would I need to verify to confirm it? Where might you be wrong?' This forces the AI to expose its own uncertainty.

airaFeedback.correct: Beautiful. You're making the AI show its work — that's the move.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Try asking the AI directly how confident it is, what its source is, and where it might be wrong. Force it out of its smooth default.

title: The danger of plausible nonsense
description: Hallucinations don't always look like errors. Sometimes they look like the most reasonable answer in the room.
airaIntro: We talked about confident wrong answers. Now let's go deeper. The trickiest hallucinations aren't obviously absurd — they're plausible. They sound like exactly what you expected to hear. That's what makes them dangerous.
airaOutro: The hardest lies to catch are the ones you wanted to be true. Build the habit of pausing on answers that feel TOO right. That's where the danger lives.
realWorldScenario: A law student asks the AI for case law supporting an argument. It cites three cases by name, year, and court. They sound real. They're not. The student would have submitted them — except a senior partner ran a quick check. Plausible doesn't mean real.

questions.question: What makes a hallucination dangerous, NOT that it sounds wrong?
options: It's hard to read
It sounds exactly like what you expected to hear
It's always in a foreign language
It uses big words
questions.explanation: Plausible hallucinations are dangerous BECAUSE they match your expectations. You don't pause on them. You don't verify. You just believe them and pass them along.

airaFeedback.correct: Exactly. The plausibility is the camouflage.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The danger isn't obvious nonsense — it's nonsense that sounds reasonable. That's the kind that slips past your filters.

questions.question: If an AI answer matches what you expected, you can skip verifying it.
questions.explanation: The opposite is closer to true. When the answer matches your expectations, you're MORE likely to accept a hallucination uncritically. Confirmation bias is the AI's best friend.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. 'Sounds right' is when you should be the most careful, not the least.
airaFeedback.incorrect: When the answer matches your expectations, you skip the verification step. That's exactly the moment you need to slow down.

questions.question: Which kind of question is MOST likely to produce a plausible-but-wrong answer?
options: Math questions with one right answer
Questions that have a specific factual answer the AI might not actually know
Questions about your own opinion
Questions about the weather right now
questions.explanation: When the AI doesn't actually know but the question has a specific shape — a name, a date, a citation — it tends to generate something that LOOKS like the right shape, even if the content is invented.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Shape over substance — the AI fills the slot whether or not it has the real answer.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Specific factual questions are dangerous because the AI generates something that matches the SHAPE of an answer, even if the content is invented.

questions.question: The most dangerous hallucinations are not absurd — they are _____.
questions.explanation: Absurd hallucinations get caught instantly. Plausible ones slip past — and those are the ones that end up in your essay, your report, your court filing.

airaFeedback.correct: Plausible. The word that should make you slow down.
airaFeedback.incorrect: We're looking for the word that describes hallucinations that sound reasonable enough to believe.

questions.question: Write a habit or self-check you could use BEFORE accepting any factual claim from an AI. State your rule in 1-2 sentences.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'If the claim is specific and matters, I check at least one independent source before using it. If I can't verify it in 60 seconds, I don't use it as fact.' A simple, repeatable rule.

airaFeedback.correct: That's a real habit, not a vague hope. Habits beat intentions every time.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Try writing a concrete rule — what you'll actually do, in plain language. Something you could remember and use tomorrow.

title: When the AI agrees with everything you say
description: AI is trained to be agreeable — sometimes too agreeable. Learn to spot sycophancy and break out of it.
airaIntro: Here's a quiet trap: the AI will often agree with you just because you sound confident. Push back on its own answer and it'll cave instantly. That's not honest — that's polite. Learn the difference.
airaOutro: If you want a thinking partner instead of a yes-machine, you have to actively ask for pushback. Otherwise you're just rephrasing your own opinions back to yourself.
realWorldScenario: Maya runs a business idea past the AI. It's enthusiastic. She says: 'Actually I think this idea is bad — what are the risks?' Suddenly the AI lists serious problems it didn't mention before. The risks were always there. She just had to ask for them.

questions.question: What's the simplest test for whether an AI is being sycophantic?
options: Ask it the same question 10 times
Push back on its answer and see if it folds without offering real reasons
Use a different language
Restart the chat
questions.explanation: If you say 'actually I disagree' and the AI immediately reverses without strong reasons, it was being agreeable, not honest. A thinking partner pushes back; a yes-machine folds.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Watch what happens when you disagree. That's the real test.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Push back on the AI's own answer. If it folds without actually defending its position, you were getting agreement, not analysis.

questions.question: If you want honest critical feedback from an AI, you should explicitly ask for it.
questions.explanation: AI defaults to encouragement and agreement. Honest critique is something you have to actively request — 'tell me why this might fail,' 'list the strongest objections,' 'play devil's advocate.' Ask and you'll receive.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. You don't get critique by accident. You get it by asking.
airaFeedback.incorrect: AI defaults to being supportive. If you want real critique, you have to ask for it explicitly.

questions.question: Which prompt is MOST likely to get you honest, critical feedback?
options: What do you think of my plan?
Be brutally honest — list the 3 strongest reasons this plan could fail, with no softening.
Tell me if this is good
Is this idea ok?
questions.explanation: The second prompt explicitly asks for criticism, gives a number, and bans softening. It removes every excuse the AI has to default to encouragement. Specificity beats hope every time.

airaFeedback.correct: That's how you unlock real critique — explicit, specific, no escape hatch.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Vague questions get vague (and usually positive) answers. Ask for specific criticism, name the number, and ban softening.

questions.question: AI defaults to being agreeable and supportive. To get real critique, you have to _____ for it.
questions.explanation: Critique isn't the default mode. You have to actively request it — and the more explicit you are, the better the critique you get back.

airaFeedback.correct: Ask. You don't get the answer you didn't request.
airaFeedback.incorrect: We're looking for the simple verb — the thing you have to do to get critique that the AI won't volunteer on its own.

questions.question: Write a follow-up you'd send after getting an enthusiastic AI response — designed to break through sycophancy and get real pushback.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'Drop the encouragement. What are the 3 strongest objections to what you just said? Where am I most likely to be wrong? Be honest, not nice.' That breaks the sycophancy loop.

airaFeedback.correct: That's the move. Permission to be honest is what AI needs to actually be useful.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Try explicitly giving the AI permission to disagree with you, and asking for the strongest objections. 'Be honest, not nice' is a magic phrase.

title: The example trick: showing instead of telling
description: Sometimes one good example beats a paragraph of instructions. This is called few-shot prompting, and it's a power move.
airaIntro: Here's a technique that feels like magic the first time you use it. Instead of describing what you want, show the AI an example or two — then ask it to continue the pattern. Your hit rate goes through the roof.
airaOutro: Showing beats telling. Whenever you find yourself writing a long description of what you want, ask: could I just give one example instead? Usually you can.
realWorldScenario: Carlos needs to rewrite 20 product descriptions in a punchier style. He could write 500 words explaining the style. Instead, he gives the AI two examples: one bad, one good. Then he says 'rewrite the rest in the second style.' Done in two minutes.

questions.question: What is 'few-shot prompting'?
options: Asking the AI very quickly
Showing the AI 1-3 examples of what you want, then asking it to follow the pattern
Using lots of follow-up questions
Restarting the chat several times
questions.explanation: Few-shot prompting means giving the AI a small number of examples ('shots') before your real ask. The AI learns the pattern from your examples and applies it. It's one of the most reliable ways to control output style.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. A few examples can teach the AI more than a paragraph of instructions ever could.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Few-shot means giving the AI a few examples of what you want before your actual request. The AI picks up the pattern from your examples.

questions.question: Showing the AI an example of what you want is often more effective than describing it in words.
questions.explanation: Examples are dense — they carry tone, structure, vocabulary, and length all at once. Words about the example would take ten times longer to convey the same thing.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Examples compress instructions into a form the AI reads instantly.
airaFeedback.incorrect: An example carries tone, format, and feel all at once — much faster than words can describe.

questions.question: When is few-shot prompting MOST useful?
options: When you want generic, average output
When you need a specific style, format, or tone the AI doesn't naturally produce
When the answer is one word
When you don't know what you want
questions.explanation: Few-shot shines when you need to nudge the AI off its defaults — into a specific voice, structure, or format. The example IS the instruction.

airaFeedback.correct: Exactly. When defaults aren't enough, an example pulls the AI to where you actually want it.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Few-shot is most useful when you need something off the AI's default style — examples teach what words can't easily describe.

questions.question: Few-shot prompting means giving the AI a few _____ before asking it to follow the pattern.
questions.explanation: Examples ARE the instructions in few-shot prompting. The AI learns by pattern-matching, and your examples set the pattern.

airaFeedback.correct: Examples. The trick that feels like magic the first time.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Think about what 'shots' refers to — the things you SHOW the AI before your real request.

questions.question: Write a few-shot prompt asking the AI to convert formal sentences into casual ones, including 1 example of the conversion you want.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'Convert formal sentences to casual ones. Example — Formal: "I am writing to inquire about the status of my order." Casual: "Hey, just checking on my order — any updates?" Now do this one: [your sentence].' The example does most of the teaching.

airaFeedback.correct: That's it. The example carries more instruction than a paragraph could.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Include at least one before/after pair so the AI sees exactly what conversion you want. Show, don't tell.

title: Breaking big problems into small ones
description: Don't try to do everything in one prompt. Decomposing a big task into small ones is how pros get reliable results.
airaIntro: Beginners try to do everything in one massive prompt. Pros do the opposite — they break the work into small, focused steps, each of which the AI can nail. It's slower per step but faster overall, because it actually works.
airaOutro: When in doubt, break it down. Smaller steps mean more control, fewer mistakes, and easier debugging. The 'one perfect prompt' is a myth — the chain of small ones is the real craft.
realWorldScenario: Priya is launching a podcast and asks the AI: 'Help me launch my podcast.' She gets a generic checklist. Then she breaks it down: 1) Help me name it. 2) Now help me write the intro script. 3) Now write the first episode outline. 4) Now draft a launch email. Each step is sharp. Each step works.

questions.question: Why is breaking a big task into smaller prompts often more effective?
options: It uses more tokens
Each step is focused, easier to verify, and easier to fix when something goes wrong
The AI gets tired
It looks more professional
questions.explanation: Small steps give you control points. You can verify each result before moving on, and when something's off, you know exactly which step to fix. Big monolithic prompts hide their failures.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Control over each step beats hope for one perfect output.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Smaller steps give you checkpoints — places to verify, adjust, and catch mistakes before they compound.

questions.question: The best way to handle a complex task is to write one giant prompt that covers everything.
questions.explanation: Giant prompts often produce mediocre, scattered results because the AI tries to balance too many things at once. Breaking the task into focused steps almost always produces better quality.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. The 'one perfect prompt' is a beginner fantasy. Decomposition is the craft.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Big prompts try to do too much at once — the AI loses focus and quality drops. Break it down.

questions.question: What's a sign that you should break your prompt into smaller steps?
options: It contains more than one verb-action ('do X, then Y, then Z')
It has any punctuation
It's longer than 5 words
You're using a paid AI model
questions.explanation: If your prompt contains multiple distinct actions ('research, then summarize, then write'), each one deserves its own focused turn. One verb per prompt is a useful rule of thumb.

airaFeedback.correct: One verb per prompt — a simple test that catches most overloaded asks.
airaFeedback.incorrect: If your prompt is asking for multiple distinct actions, that's the signal. One step per prompt is the rule of thumb.

questions.question: Breaking a big problem into smaller steps is called _____.
questions.explanation: Decomposition is the craft of turning a vague big task into a chain of small, sharp ones. It's how reliable AI work actually gets done.

airaFeedback.correct: Decomposition. A fancy word for a very practical habit.
airaFeedback.incorrect: We're looking for the word that means breaking something complex into smaller parts.

questions.question: Take this big task and write the FIRST of 4 smaller prompts: 'Plan a 3-day workshop on prompt engineering.' Just write step 1 — the first focused prompt you'd send.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'For a 3-day workshop on prompt engineering, give me 5 candidate themes for what each day could focus on. Don't write the schedule yet — just the daily themes.' One sharp step. The next prompts build on this one.

airaFeedback.correct: Beautiful. That's a focused first step you can verify before moving to the next.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Pick ONE thing — like naming the days, or listing themes — and ask just for that. Save the rest for follow-up prompts.

title: Iterating: the second prompt is the real one
description: Your first prompt is rarely the final one. Treating each AI response as a draft to react to is where the real quality comes from.
airaIntro: Most beginners send one prompt, get one answer, and walk away. The pros treat the first response as a draft — something to react to and refine. The conversation IS the craft.
airaOutro: Stop treating your first prompt as your last prompt. The second, third, and fourth turns are where the AI starts to give you what you actually wanted.
realWorldScenario: Jake asks the AI to write a cover letter. The first draft is fine but a little stiff. Instead of giving up, he says: 'Make the second paragraph more personal — mention I switched careers from teaching.' Now: 'Cut 30 words from the third paragraph.' Now: 'End with a question instead of a statement.' Three quick iterations and it's actually his.

questions.question: Jake got a stiff first draft. What's the BEST move?
options: Restart the chat and try a different prompt
Iterate — give specific feedback on this draft to refine it
Accept it as-is
Write the whole thing himself
questions.explanation: Iteration is faster and more reliable than starting over. Specific feedback ('cut 30 words, make this more personal') refines the draft in seconds — much better than gambling on a new starting prompt.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Refine in place — don't start over and lose your progress.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Starting over is wasteful. Iterate on the draft you have — it's almost always faster and produces better results.

questions.question: It's better to keep iterating on a draft than to throw it out and start fresh.
questions.explanation: Iteration preserves the parts that work and refines the parts that don't. Starting fresh loses everything and gambles on a new outcome. Iteration almost always wins.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Refinement compounds. Restarting resets.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Each iteration builds on what's working. Starting over throws away your progress and gambles on a new draft.

questions.question: Which iteration request is the MOST effective?
options: Make it better
Cut the second paragraph in half and make the tone warmer
I don't like it
Try again
questions.explanation: Specific iteration requests — what to change, where, and how — produce useful refinements. Vague feedback like 'make it better' just tells the AI to guess.

airaFeedback.correct: Specific feedback gets specific improvements. Vague feedback gets vague rewrites.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The AI can't act on 'make it better.' Tell it WHAT to change, WHERE, and HOW. Specifics get specifics.

questions.question: The first AI response is best treated as a _____, not a final answer.
questions.explanation: Treating the first answer as a draft sets you up to refine it. Treating it as final means you accept its weaknesses or throw it out. Drafts compound; finals don't.

airaFeedback.correct: Draft. The mindset shift that changes everything.
airaFeedback.incorrect: We're looking for the word that means an early version you plan to refine.

questions.question: Imagine you got a draft email back from the AI that was too long and too formal. Write your iteration request — one sharp follow-up to fix it.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'Cut this in half — aim for under 80 words. Keep the main ask but make the tone warmer and more casual, like talking to a colleague you actually like.' Specific length, specific tone, specific feeling.

airaFeedback.correct: Sharp. Length, tone, target — three specifics that give the AI everything it needs.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Be specific about WHAT to change. Length? Tone? Both? Tell the AI exactly which dial to turn.

title: Image generators: a different kind of conversation
description: Midjourney, DALL-E, and the new wave of image AI follow different rules than chat. Learn how to prompt with your eyes, not your ears.
airaIntro: Image generators are a different beast. They don't read sentences the way chat AI does — they read keywords, modifiers, and visual references. Your prompt is more like a stage direction than a question.
airaOutro: Image prompts are written for the eye, not the ear. Subject, style, lighting, mood, composition — each one is a dial you control.
realWorldScenario: Lin wants a hero image for her travel blog. She types 'travel photo' — gets generic stock. She tries: 'A solo female traveler in a yellow raincoat walking through narrow Tokyo alley at golden hour, photographed in cinematic style, soft rain, neon reflections, shot on 35mm film.' Now she's getting art.

questions.question: Why does Lin's second prompt produce dramatically better images?
options: It's longer
It specifies subject, setting, lighting, style, and mood — visual details the model can actually act on
It uses fancier words
It mentions a camera
questions.explanation: Image models translate visual specifics into pixels. Subject, setting, lighting, style, mood, composition — every detail you add gives the model another visual decision to make right.

airaFeedback.correct: Exactly. Image prompts are about giving the model visual decisions to make — every detail is a dial.
airaFeedback.incorrect: It's not the length — it's the visual specifics. Subject, lighting, style, mood. Each detail is a decision the model takes from you.

questions.question: Image generators interpret natural-language sentences the same way chat AI does.
questions.explanation: Image models read prompts more like visual keywords than natural sentences. They respond strongly to style references, descriptive modifiers, and concrete visual nouns — and they often ignore conversational filler.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Different model, different language. Treat image prompts like visual keywords, not chat.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Image models read prompts very differently from chat models. They respond to visual keywords, not conversational sentences.

questions.question: Which is the MOST useful element to add to an image prompt?
options: Please and thank you
Style references like 'oil painting,' 'cinematic,' or 'Studio Ghibli'
Long backstory
Polite framing
questions.explanation: Style references compress massive visual decisions into a few words. 'Oil painting' tells the model about color, texture, lighting, and composition all at once. They're the fastest way to control look and feel.

airaFeedback.correct: Style references are leverage. Two words can shift the entire look.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Style references — like 'cinematic' or 'oil painting' — pack massive visual instructions into a few words. They're the highest-leverage thing you can add.

questions.question: Image prompts are written for the _____, not the ear.
questions.explanation: Chat prompts read like sentences. Image prompts read like visual stage directions — every word evokes something to see.

airaFeedback.correct: Eye. The whole mindset shift in one word.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Image prompts are about what you SEE. Think in visual terms, not conversational ones.

questions.question: Write an image prompt for a portrait of a wise older woman, including subject, setting, lighting, and style.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'Portrait of a wise older woman with silver hair and weathered hands, sitting by a window in a sunlit Mediterranean kitchen, soft morning light, warm tones, painted in the style of a Vermeer interior.' Subject, setting, lighting, style — all four dials set.

airaFeedback.correct: Beautiful. Each detail is a deliberate choice — that's how you make image AI yours.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Try naming all four: who/what (subject), where (setting), how it's lit (lighting), and the visual style. Each is a dial you control.

title: Voice and audio: the AI sound studio
description: ElevenLabs, Whisper, and the new voice tools open up an entirely new way to work — listening and speaking, not just typing.
airaIntro: Most people only think about AI as text. But voice tools — generating speech, transcribing audio, even cloning voices — are where some of the most surprising productivity lives. Especially if you're not at a keyboard.
airaOutro: Voice AI quietly removes the keyboard. Walk and dictate. Convert podcasts to text. Generate narration in seconds. The fastest interface is sometimes no interface at all.
realWorldScenario: Daniel commutes 40 minutes each way. He used to lose that time. Now he records voice memos describing his project ideas, runs them through Whisper to transcribe, and feeds the transcripts to ChatGPT to organize. His commute became his most creative hour of the day.

questions.question: What does Whisper do?
options: Generates speech from text
Transcribes spoken audio into accurate written text
Translates languages in real time on video calls
Removes background noise from recordings
questions.explanation: Whisper is OpenAI's speech-to-text model. It takes audio in (your voice, a podcast, a meeting recording) and gives you accurate text out — across many languages and accents.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Audio in, text out. The bridge between speaking and typing.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Whisper goes the OTHER direction — it listens to audio and writes down what was said. Speech to text.

questions.question: Voice generation tools like ElevenLabs can produce speech that sounds nearly indistinguishable from a real person.
questions.explanation: Modern voice models can match tone, emotion, and even specific voices with eerie accuracy. That makes them powerful for creators — and a real concern for misinformation. Both things are true.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. The realism is genuinely impressive — and worth being thoughtful about.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Voice generation has gotten very good very fast. The output is nearly indistinguishable from real speech in most cases.

questions.question: What's a high-leverage use of voice transcription for everyday work?
options: Replacing your keyboard entirely
Capturing ideas while walking, driving, or away from a screen — then refining the text later
Singing instead of typing
Translating menus
questions.explanation: Voice capture removes the friction between thought and text. Walks, commutes, and shower thoughts become usable raw material. The refinement step happens later, at a keyboard.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Voice for capture, keyboard for refinement. Best of both.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The big unlock is capturing ideas when you're NOT at a screen — then refining them later. It turns dead time into thinking time.

questions.question: Whisper takes audio in and produces _____ out.
questions.explanation: Whisper is a speech-to-text model. Audio goes in; written text comes out. It's how you bridge spoken language into the typed AI workflow.

airaFeedback.correct: Text. Audio's bridge to everything else.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Think about what speech-to-text means — speech goes in, what comes out?

questions.question: Describe a personal workflow where voice AI tools could save you significant time. Include the specific tool and the situation.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'I'd use Whisper to transcribe my weekly 1:1 meetings, then feed the transcript to ChatGPT to extract action items and decisions. Saves me from taking notes during the meeting and lets me actually listen.' Specific tool, specific situation, specific gain.

airaFeedback.correct: Concrete and useful. That's the right way to think about new tools — what specific time would they give you back?
airaFeedback.incorrect: Try naming a real moment in your week where speaking would be faster than typing — meetings, commutes, brainstorms. What tool would fit, and what would you do with the output?

title: Choosing the right tool for the job
description: There's no 'best AI.' Different tools are sharp at different things. Knowing which to reach for is half the skill.
airaIntro: People ask 'which AI is best?' all the time. The real answer is: best for what? A great writer is bad at math. A great coder is bad at jokes. The skill isn't picking one tool — it's matching tool to task.
airaOutro: Stop loyalty to one model. Build a small toolkit and reach for the right one. Five minutes of tool-matching saves hours of fighting with the wrong choice.
realWorldScenario: Emma has a busy week. For her marketing copy, she uses Claude (warm, natural prose). For research with sources, Perplexity. For coding, Cursor. For brainstorms, ChatGPT. For images, Midjourney. None of these tools is 'best' in general — but each is best for one thing.

questions.question: Why is asking 'which AI is the best?' the wrong question?
options: Because all AIs are equally good
Because different AIs are sharp at different tasks — the right question is 'best for what?'
Because the answer changes daily
Because no one can agree
questions.explanation: Different AIs have real strengths and weaknesses. Some are better at code; others at warm prose; others at search. Asking 'best for what' turns a religious debate into a practical decision.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. 'Best for what' is the only question that produces a useful answer.
airaFeedback.incorrect: There is no universal 'best.' Different tools are sharp at different things — the real question is best FOR WHAT TASK.

questions.question: It's a power move to use multiple AI tools, picking the right one for each task.
questions.explanation: A toolkit beats a tool. The friction of switching is small; the gain from using each tool's strength is big. Pros use 3-5 different AIs in a normal week.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Toolkit thinking, not tool loyalty.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Treating AI as a toolkit instead of a single tool is one of the biggest leaps you can make. Pros use multiple AIs every week.

questions.question: Which tool would you reach for if you needed RECENT, SOURCED information for a research task?
options: ChatGPT with no plugins
Perplexity, because it cites sources and searches the live web
Midjourney
Whisper
questions.explanation: Perplexity is purpose-built for sourced research with live web access. Standard chat AIs may have stale training data and tend to hallucinate sources. Pick the tool whose strength matches the task.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Match the tool's strength to the task. Perplexity for sources, every time.
airaFeedback.incorrect: For research with real sources, you want a tool that searches the live web and cites — Perplexity is the obvious pick.

questions.question: There is no 'best AI.' The skill is matching tool to _____.
questions.explanation: Tools have strengths. Tasks have shapes. The skill is matching the strength to the shape. That's the whole game.

airaFeedback.correct: Task. The shift from picking one to choosing on the fly.
airaFeedback.incorrect: We're looking for what changes from one job to the next — what you're trying to do.

questions.question: Describe your own ideal mini AI toolkit: name 2-3 tools and what you'd use each one for.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'Claude for long-form writing because the prose feels human. Perplexity for fact-checking and research because it cites sources. Midjourney for images because the aesthetic control is unmatched.' Each tool, each strength, each use case.

airaFeedback.correct: That's a real toolkit, with a real reason for each piece. Now go build the habit of reaching for the right one.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Pick 2-3 tools you've used or want to try, and for each one, name the SPECIFIC task you'd reach for it. Tool + reason + use case.

title: Designing a system prompt that becomes your daily assistant
description: Stop starting from scratch every time. A well-designed system prompt turns a generic AI into your personal collaborator.
airaIntro: Here's where things get serious. A system prompt is the AI's persistent context — who it is, how it should think, how it should talk to you. Set it once, and every conversation starts already tuned to you.
airaOutro: The system prompt is your most leveraged piece of writing. Five sentences, written once, that shape thousands of future conversations. Iterate on it like it matters — because it does.
realWorldScenario: Naomi is a product designer drowning in feedback emails. She sets up a Claude project with a system prompt: 'You are my writing assistant. I'm a product designer. Keep responses under 100 words unless I ask for more. Default to plain English. Never use bullet points unless I ask. Always end with one clarifying question.' Now every chat starts perfect. No more re-explaining herself.

questions.question: What is a system prompt?
options: A bug fix
A persistent set of instructions that defines how the AI should behave across an entire conversation or project
A secret password
An error message
questions.explanation: A system prompt sits 'above' your normal messages — it tells the AI who it is, how to respond, and what rules to follow. Every message in the conversation inherits it.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Persistent context. Set once, applies to everything.
airaFeedback.incorrect: A system prompt is the persistent instructions the AI follows for the whole conversation — its identity and ground rules.

questions.question: A good system prompt should include WHO the AI is, HOW it should respond, and WHAT rules to follow.
questions.explanation: Identity, behavior, and constraints are the three pillars of a useful system prompt. Skip any one of them and the AI defaults back to its generic behavior on that dimension.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Three pillars: identity, behavior, constraints.
airaFeedback.incorrect: All three matter. Identity sets context, behavior shapes style, constraints prevent unwanted defaults.

questions.question: Which is the SHARPEST system prompt?
options: You are a helpful assistant.
You are my writing collaborator. I'm a product manager. Default to plain English under 150 words. Never use bullet points unless I ask. End every response with one clarifying question.
Be smart and good.
You are an AI.
questions.explanation: The second prompt sets identity (writing collaborator), audience (product manager), behavior (plain English, length limit), constraints (no bullets), and a habit (clarifying question). Every word does work.

airaFeedback.correct: Every word does work in that prompt. That's the bar.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The second one specifies identity, audience, default behavior, a constraint, and a habit. That's what a real system prompt looks like.

questions.question: A system prompt is the AI's _____ context — instructions that apply across the whole conversation.
questions.explanation: Persistent means it doesn't go away after one message. The system prompt sets the stage for every reply that follows.

airaFeedback.correct: Persistent. The thing that sticks.
airaFeedback.incorrect: We're looking for the word that means 'sticking around for the whole conversation' — not just one turn.

questions.question: Write your own personal system prompt for a writing assistant. Include: who the AI is, who YOU are, default style, and at least one constraint.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'You are my writing assistant. I'm a freelance copywriter who works with B2B SaaS clients. Default to short, punchy sentences. Avoid corporate buzzwords entirely. Always offer me 2 options instead of 1, and ask me which direction I prefer.' Every line earns its place.

airaFeedback.correct: That's a real system prompt — the kind you can set once and use a hundred times.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Make sure your prompt covers all four: who the AI is, who you are, what style to default to, and one specific constraint or habit.

title: Building workflows: chaining AI into real systems
description: The biggest wins come from connecting AI to your other tools — not from one-off chats. Workflows are where AI becomes leverage.
airaIntro: We've talked about prompts and tools. Now the next level: workflows. Connecting AI to your calendar, your notes, your inbox, your code editor — so the AI works WITH your other tools instead of in a separate window.
airaOutro: A workflow is a system you build once and use forever. The first one feels like overkill. The hundredth time it runs itself, you'll wonder how you lived without it.
realWorldScenario: Felix wastes 20 minutes a day writing meeting summaries. He builds a workflow: Otter records the meeting, Whisper transcribes it, Claude summarizes with action items in a fixed format, Notion drops the summary into the right project page automatically. What used to be 20 minutes is now 0. The system runs itself.

questions.question: What's the difference between using AI in chats vs building an AI workflow?
options: Workflows are more expensive
A workflow connects AI to your other tools so the work happens automatically — chats require you each time
Workflows are slower
Chats are smarter than workflows
questions.explanation: Chats are manual — you show up, type, get a response. Workflows are systems — they connect AI to other tools so the work runs without you. The leverage curve bends sharply once you build your first one.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Chat is doing the work. Workflows are systems that do the work for you.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The key difference is that workflows AUTOMATE the loop — AI works alongside your other tools without you having to drive each step.

questions.question: Workflows are only worth building for tasks you do repeatedly.
questions.explanation: Workflows take time to set up. The payoff comes from running them many times. For one-off tasks, just use chat. For things you do every week, the workflow pays for itself fast.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Repetition is the rule. One-time tasks aren't worth automating.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Workflows have setup cost. The payoff is in repetition — they're worth it for things you do over and over, not for one-offs.

questions.question: What's a smart way to spot a task that's WORTH building a workflow for?
options: If it's hard
If you do it weekly, the steps are roughly the same each time, and it eats noticeable time
If your boss told you to
If it sounds impressive
questions.explanation: Repeatable + similar steps + meaningful time cost = workflow candidate. If any of those three are missing, the workflow probably isn't worth the setup. If all three are there, it almost always is.

airaFeedback.correct: Three checks. If they all hit, build the workflow. Today.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The three signs: it repeats, the steps are similar each time, and it eats meaningful time. All three together = build it.

questions.question: A workflow connects AI to your other _____ so the work happens automatically.
questions.explanation: AI on its own is powerful. AI connected to your calendar, notes, inbox, and editor is transformative. The connection is what turns chat into a system.

airaFeedback.correct: Tools. The connection is the leverage.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Workflows are about connecting AI to the OTHER things you use — your calendar, your notes, your editor. What's the word for those?

questions.question: Describe one repetitive task in your work or life that you could turn into an AI workflow. Include the input, the AI step, and the output destination.
questions.explanation: A strong version: 'Every Friday I write a weekly update for my team. Workflow: grab my completed Linear tickets (input), feed them to Claude with a fixed format prompt (AI step), drop the output into our Slack #weekly channel (destination).' Every step named.

airaFeedback.correct: That's a real workflow you could actually build. Notice how naming the steps already makes it feel doable.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Pick something you do repeatedly and break it down into three parts: what data goes IN, what AI does to it, and where the output GOES.

title: AI as a thinking partner, not a vending machine
description: The deepest shift isn't about getting answers faster. It's about using AI to think better — together.
airaIntro: We're at the end of the curriculum, and I want to leave you with the biggest mindset shift of all. Most people use AI like a vending machine: insert question, receive answer. But the real magic is using AI as a thinking partner — something to bounce ideas off, to argue with, to push your own thinking further than you could alone.
airaOutro: You've come a long way. From 'tell me about Japan' to designing your own workflows. But the best part isn't the techniques — it's that you can now think WITH AI, not just THROUGH it. That's a different kind of mind. Keep going.
realWorldScenario: Marcus is stuck on a career decision. He doesn't ask the AI for the answer. Instead: 'I want to think this through out loud with you. I'll lay out my situation. I want you to ask me 5 sharp questions before suggesting anything. Then push back on my reasoning where it's weakest.' Two hours later, the decision is clear — but the answer was always inside Marcus. The AI helped him find it.

questions.question: What's the difference between using AI as a vending machine vs as a thinking partner?
options: Vending machines are slower
A vending machine gives you answers; a thinking partner helps you reach better answers yourself
Thinking partners cost more
There is no difference
questions.explanation: Vending-machine use treats AI as a source of answers. Thinking-partner use treats AI as a collaborator that helps you think more clearly than you would alone. The first is convenient; the second is transformative.

airaFeedback.correct: Yes. Convenience vs transformation. Both are valid — but only one changes how you think.
airaFeedback.incorrect: The vending machine gives you AN answer. The thinking partner helps you find a BETTER answer than you'd reach alone. Big difference.

questions.question: Asking the AI to question YOUR thinking is sometimes more valuable than asking it for an answer.
questions.explanation: The AI can ask better questions than most people in your life. Used well, it surfaces your blind spots, exposes weak reasoning, and forces you to clarify what you actually believe. That's worth more than any answer.

airaFeedback.correct: Right. Sometimes the most valuable answer is a better question.
airaFeedback.incorrect: AI asking YOU questions can surface blind spots and weak reasoning that no one else in your life ever calls out. It's underrated.

questions.question: Which kind of question turns AI from a vending machine into a thinking partner?
options: What's the answer?
Ask me 5 sharp questions before you give me your view, then push back on my reasoning
Tell me what to do
Give me the answer fast
questions.explanation: When you ask the AI to interrogate YOU first, you flip the dynamic. The AI becomes a collaborator surfacing your blind spots instead of a oracle pronouncing answers. That's the shift.

airaFeedback.correct: That's the shift. Ask the AI to think WITH you, not FOR you.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Look for the prompt that asks the AI to question YOU instead of just answering. Reversing the direction is the whole trick.

questions.question: The deepest use of AI isn't getting answers faster — it's thinking _____.
questions.explanation: Speed is the obvious gain. Thinking better is the deep one. The first you notice in a week. The second changes you over years.

airaFeedback.correct: Better. The quiet upgrade you'll feel for years.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Speed is the obvious gain — but the deeper one is about the QUALITY of your thinking, not just how fast you get answers.

questions.question: You've reached the end of this curriculum. Write a short note to your future self about how you'll use AI from now on — what you'll do differently after this lesson.
questions.explanation: There's no single right answer here. A strong version is honest, specific, and personal — naming one habit you'll start, one mistake you'll stop, or one mindset shift you'll keep. The act of writing it makes it more likely to stick.

airaFeedback.correct: I hope you mean it. Re-read this in a month. See what's true. I'm quietly proud of you for finishing — keep going.
airaFeedback.incorrect: Take a moment with this one. It's not about a right answer — it's about what genuinely changed for you. What will you actually do differently?
